retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: CAD remains the number 1 cause of death in the United States. Given the prevalence of CAD and its risk factors, interprofessional, team-based care may result in significantly improved patient outcomes. [41] Clinicians should be aware of screening recommendations and the impact of risk factor mitigation on CAD outcomes. Nurses play a central role in routine screening and education. Clinical pharmacists play a pivotal role in the pharmacologic management of modifiable risk factors such as hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and smoking cessation. Nutritionists contribute by providing dietary education. Community outreach by all team members can help mitigate the complex yet crucial role that socioeconomic status can have on CAD risk and outcomes. This section covers screening recommendations, the impact of risk factor mitigation on CAD outcomes, aspirin, and new CAD screening tests.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension: About 1 out of every 3 patients have hypertension. Hypertension and smoking were responsible for the largest number of deaths in a 2009 review comparing twelve modifiable risk factors. [20] Yet, only 54% of these patients achieve adequate blood pressure control. [21] Hypertension has long been a major risk factor for heart disease through both oxidative and mechanical stress it places on the arterial wall. [22] [23] A 1996 article reported that in the Framingham cohort, a systolic of 20 mmHg and diastolic of 10 mmHg increase was observed from age 30 to 65 years. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol - Chronic alcohol use and heavy drinking are risk factors for stroke. J-shaped association between alcohol and ischemic stroke risk has been suggested by most studies. There is a protective effect from light to moderate consumption (likely due to an increase in HDL and a decrease in platelet aggregation) and increased risk of stroke with heavy alcohol consumption (due to a hypercoagulable state, alcohol-induced hypertension, cardiomyopathy, and Afib). Stopping or reducing alcohol consumption is recommended for patients with ischemic stroke or TIA. [5] [8] Lower socioeconomic status , including low income, lower education level, is associated with an increased risk of stroke recurrence. [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Obesity: 69% of adults in the United States are overweight or obese. 35% of adults are obese. [18] Obesity is an independent risk factor for CAD and also increases the risk of developing other CAD risk factors, including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes mellitus. [29] [30] [31] One recent study indicated that obese patients were twice as likely to have coronary heart disease (hazard ratio 2.00, 95% CI 1.67-2.40) after adjustment for demographics, smoking, physical activity, and alcohol intake. [32] A 1998 research study and 2016 review article conferred that obesity is associated with more complex, raised, and hi-grade atherosclerotic coronary artery lesions. [33] [34] The "obesity paradox" has also been reported. Despite evidence pointing to obesity as an independent risk factor for cardiovascular morbidity, some authors have described better outcomes in overweight and obese patients. There is an ongoing debate in light of this conflicting data. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين), indicating that individuals who consume cocaine are at risk for damage to the arterial walls. This relationship highlights the vascular complications of cocaine consumption and underscores the importance of monitoring for arterial injury in patients with a history of cocaine use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with both unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and arterial dissection (تسلخ الشرايين). This interconnection indicates that exposure to cocaine can precipitate acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina as well as serious vascular injuries like arterial dissection. Recognizing cocaine use as a contributing factor is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of patients presenting with these cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي (arteriosclerosis) is clinically associated with several findings and risk factors, including النفخة الانقباضية (a systolic murmur), تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use), حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance), عرج (limp or gait disturbance), and لغط الشريان السباتي (carotid bruit). In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم (generalized arteriosclerosis), reflecting a more extensive form of the disease. Recognizing these associations aids clinicians in identifying arteriosclerotic disease, assessing related vascular signs, and emphasizing preventive measures such as routine health maintenance and smoking cessation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) is clinically associated with renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ), routine health maintenance (حفظ الصحة الروتيني), and the presence of a limp (عرج). Conversely, renal dysfunction is also clinically associated with arteriosclerosis, highlighting a bidirectional relationship between vascular stiffening and kidney impairment. The systolic murmur may reflect hemodynamic alterations caused by stiffened arterial walls, while tobacco use is a well‑known risk factor that accelerates the development and progression of arteriosclerotic changes. Routine health maintenance practices are important for early detection and management of these interrelated conditions. A limp can arise from peripheral arterial disease secondary to arteriosclerosis, underscoring the systemic impact of arterial pathology. Recognizing and addressing these interconnected associations is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use is clinically associated with both arterial erosion and increased blood pressure. The vascular damage manifested as arterial erosion reflects the harmful effects of cocaine on the arterial wall, while the stimulant properties of cocaine contribute to elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these interrelated complications is important for clinicians when evaluating patients who use cocaine, as they may require monitoring for vascular injury and hypertension and appropriate interventions to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2186
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) and with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني). It also commonly co‑occurs with vascular stiffness (رأب الوعاء). These interconnections indicate that patients with coronary artery disease often have underlying cardiac abnormalities, are at heightened risk for developing congestive heart failure, and frequently exhibit vascular stiffness, all of which are important considerations for diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1932
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي (arteriosclerosis) is linked clinically to several findings and risk factors, including النفخة الانقباضية (systolic murmur), تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use), حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance), عرج (limp), and لغط فخذي (thigh bruit). It is also associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال, indicating a broader clinical relationship. In the disease hierarchy, التصلب العصيدي is classified as a child of التصلب العصيدي المعمم (generalized arteriosclerosis) and as a descendant of مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد (chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease). Vascular ischemia is reported to have a direct relationship with التصلب العصيدي, underscoring the role of arterial narrowing in ischemic processes. Additionally, التصلب العصيدي is related to مجازة الشريان التاجي (coronary artery graft), reflecting its relevance in coronary revascularization strategies. Together, these interconnections highlight the multifaceted clinical presentation, etiological factors, and therapeutic considerations associated with arteriosclerotic disease.

Additionally, اضطراب قلبي وعائي also lists اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَدم (SMQ) as one of its members, underscoring the central role of myocardial inflammation across cardiovascular disorders. Overall, the knowledge network connects broad cardiovascular disease categories (مرض تأموري) to specific vascular pathologies, venous disorders, arterial events, inflammatory heart conditions, and related clinical manifestations such as chest pain and hypertension, while highlighting cocaine use as a notable risk factor for arterial dissection and hypertension. This integrated view supports comprehensive assessment and management of patients with overlapping circulatory and cardiac disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: triglycerides are contraindicated in hypertriglyceridemia. Additional pathologies localized to the coronary arterial tree include coronary artery stenosis (strictuur; coronair) associated with arterial narrowing (التَضَيُّق), coronary artery spasm (تشنج الشريان التاجي), coronary arterial atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في التاجي), and coronary thrombosis (خثار الشريان التاجي). These vascular abnormalities impair blood flow within the coronary arterial system (أم دم الشريان التاجي) and collectively constitute coronary disease (مرض التاجي). Understanding the relationships among these entities is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of appropriate interventional or medical therapies in patients with coronary artery disease.
